metadata.working_title: miniML: A Deep Learning Framework for Automated and Generalized Synaptic Event Analysis

abstract_sketch.problem: Spontaneous synaptic events carry fundamental information about synaptic function and plasticity, but their stochastic nature and low SNR make detection difficult.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing template- and threshold-based methods suffer from strong threshold dependence, produce false positives, and require laborious manual inspection.
abstract_sketch.approach: We introduce miniML, a CNN-LSTM classifier trained on ~30,000 labeled cerebellar mEPSC segments that classifies short windows and detects events via sliding window inference, coupled with transfer learning for generalization.
abstract_sketch.key_results: miniML achieves 98.4% validation accuracy, outperforms template matching, deconvolution, Bayesian inference, SimplyFire, MiniAnalysis, and finite-threshold methods in F1 across SNRs, generalizes across mouse/rat/zebrafish/Drosophila/human iPSC preparations, and detects ~40% of optical mEPSPs in ASAP5 voltage imaging data (recall 0.38 vs 0.25 template, 0.09 deconvolution).
abstract_sketch.impact: Automated, threshold-robust, and reproducible synaptic event analysis applicable to electrophysiology and optical imaging, enabling high-throughput analysis of neural function and dysfunction.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Synaptic communication underlies brain function; spontaneous miniature events reveal receptor state and plasticity
Miniature EPSCs/EPSPs are small and stochastic, producing low SNR that challenges reliable detection
Existing techniques (finite-difference, template-matching, deconvolution, Bayesian, peak finding) are threshold-sensitive and require manual review
Deep learning, particularly CNNs, has transformed 1D biological signal analysis; CNN-LSTM architectures exploit temporal structure
We present miniML, a supervised CNN-LSTM classifier that detects synaptic events with high precision and generalizes across preparations via transfer learning

subsections.title: miniML enables highly accurate classification of synaptic events
content_bullets: CNN-LSTM-dense model trained on ~30,000 labeled MF-GC mEPSC windows (21,140 recorded + 9,000 simulated)
Best model achieves 98.4% validation accuracy (SD 0.1, fivefold CV)
Performance saturates above 5,000 samples (<0.2% gain)
Saliency maps localize discrimination to event peak; UMAP shows linear separability

subsections.title: miniML robustly detects events in electrophysiological recordings
content_bullets: Sliding-window inference with stride for fast analysis
120-s, 50-kHz recording analyzed in ~15 s on laptop (Apple M1 Pro, 16 GB RAM) with stride 20
Output in [0,1] interpretable as event confidence; peak threshold 0.5
GPU enables <20 s runtime for 6,000,000-sample trace

subsections.title: AI-based event detection is superior to previous methods
content_bullets: Synthetic events on event-free traces provide ground truth; log-normal amplitudes, SNR 2-15 dB
Compared against template-matching, deconvolution, finite-threshold, Bayesian, SimplyFire, MiniAnalysis
miniML had highest F1 across SNRs and highest precision (zero false positives)
miniML remained consistent across threshold range 5-195%; false positives only at 5%

subsections.title: miniML reliably detects events in out-of-sample preparations
content_bullets: Calyx of Held mEPSCs (P9) detected with pretrained MF-GC model
Cerebellar Golgi cells: slower decay 1.83 ms (SD 0.44, n=10) vs GC training 0.9 ms; still detected reliably
Human iPSC-derived cortical glutamatergic neurons: 0.15 Hz (SD 0.24, n=56); events in ~51% of neurons

subsections.title: Generalization via transfer learning
content_bullets: Freeze CNN layers, retrain LSTM+dense; as few as 400 samples suffice
TL accuracy 95.4% vs 96.1% for full training with 10x data
Applied to MF-GC mEPSPs: voltage-clamp 0.49 Hz (SD 0.53), current-clamp 0.54 Hz (SD 0.6), n=15, strongly correlated

subsections.title: Diverse event kinetics in adult zebrafish telencephalon
content_bullets: TL model detects spontaneous EPSCs in n=34 zebrafish principal neurons
Decay times uncorrelated with anatomical position (p>0.05 in all 3 dims)
Mean decay and rise times correlated across neurons; input resistance negatively correlated with decay time

subsections.title: Genetic receptor perturbation at Drosophila NMJ
content_bullets: WT and GluRIIA mutant two-electrode voltage-clamp recordings analyzed via TL
GluRIIA: mEPSC amplitude reduced 54%, frequency reduced 64%
Half decay -58%, rise -18% in GluRIIA vs WT

subsections.title: Optical event detection with iGluSnFR3 and ASAP5
content_bullets: iGluSnFR3: n=1524 ROIs, rise 21.8 ms median, half decay 48.7 ms median; power-law frequency distribution
ASAP5-Kv voltage imaging: SNR 2.26 (SD 0.46) vs electrophysiology 4.79 (SD 0.74), n=5
miniML recall 0.38+/-0.05 vs template 0.25+/-0.05 (Cohen's d 1.25) vs deconvolution 0.09+/-0.01 (d 3.87); precision 0.93/0.95/0.8

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: miniML surpasses existing methods in precision and F1, eliminating trade-off between false positives and negatives
Threshold robustness supports automated, reproducible pipelines
Generalizes across species, preparations, and modalities via TL with a few hundred labeled events
Extends to optical recordings (iGluSnFR3, ASAP5) where template methods fail
Limitations: supervised labeling burden; overlapping event separation; imaging SNR/temporal resolution constraints
Outlook: unsupervised autoencoders, wavelet/shapelet augmentation, clinical neurophysiology extensions

sections.title: Materials and Methods
content_bullets: Animals: C57BL/6J mice 1-5 months (Calyx P9); ACSF and internal solution composition
Recording: HEKA EPC10, 50 kHz, filter 2.9 kHz; LJP +13 mV; 120-s sweeps
Training data: mEPSCs from Delvendahl et al. 2019; 600-sample windows (12 ms at 50 kHz); 4500 augmentation negatives + simulated events -> 30,140 total (21,140 recorded, 9,000 simulated)
Architecture: 3 conv blocks (leaky ReLU, batch norm, avg pool) + 4th conv block + bidirectional LSTM + dense + dropout + sigmoid; 190,913 trainable params
Training: TensorFlow 2.12, Adam+AMSGrad, eta=2e-5, batch 128, 100 epochs max, early stopping patience 8; ~8 min on NVIDIA Tesla P100
Inference: sliding window stride, min peak height 0.5, 10-stride width; biexponential synthetic events, log-normal amplitudes variance 0.4, decay mean 1.0 ms (SD 0.25 ms)
Transfer learning: freeze convs, lr 2e-8, batch 32, dropout 0.5, patience 15

Baselines: template-matching thr -4, deconvolution 5*SD, finite-threshold -4 pA, Bayesian min amp 3.5 noise phi=[0.90,-0.52] rate 0.5 threshold 6*SD, SimplyFire kernel 300 stride 100 min amp 4 pA, MiniAnalysis auto EPSC mode
ASAP5: 400 fps, 482/18 ex, 525/50 em, 46 mW/mm^2 irradiance; electrophysiology 10 kHz acquisition, 1 kHz low-pass
Stats: permutation t-test 5000 reshuffles; Cohen's d or mean diff with 95% BCa CI from 5000 bootstraps
